full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|2.50|> I have a friend who is a retired priest, and he loves to tell the story of his first Pentecost as a rector.<|11.68|> <|11.68|> He wanted to illustrate the drama of that rush of violent wind that filled the entire house<|18.92|> <|18.92|> where the disciples and others were waiting and sitting.<|23.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> To accomplish this, he rented an enormous industrial fan and positioned it in the choir loft.<|7.78|> <|8.24|> It was aimed right down at the congregation.<|11.20|> <|12.12|> At just the right moment in his sermon, when he had built to a crescendo of Holy Spirit vigor, a helper turned on the fan.<|22.12|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It was dramatic, all right. Hats flew off women's heads. Bulletins scattered all over the place. People shrieked and grabbed for their belongings. It was terrifying. It was chaos.<|14.66|> <|15.80|> He likes to say that he has since always gone with the still, small voice version of God's spiritual manifestation.<|26.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But I actually think he was on to something with the giant fan.<|6.30|> <|6.30|> I think that illustration was exactly right.<|9.94|> <|9.94|> It was disruptive.<|11.44|> <|11.44|> It was attention-getting.<|13.18|> <|13.18|> And it could not be ignored.<|15.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The followers of Jesus, men and women, as described by the writer of Acts, were shaken from their anxious waiting by that wind.<|12.88|> <|13.34|> Like a rush of violent wind accompanied by tongues as of fire, which rested on each of them, they received the gift of the Holy Spirit.<|25.40|> <|25.40|> Amen.<|25.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This disturbance was so great that it drew the attention of Jews from every nation who<|8.04|> <|8.04|> were living in Jerusalem.<|11.30|> <|11.30|> And one of the distinct benefits of preaching today is that I did not have to pronounce all<|17.72|> <|17.72|> of the nationalities listed in the reading as Broderick did.<|22.80|> <|22.80|> Let's simply say they were Jews from throughout the Mediterranean world.<|28.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> beyond. This wind, these tongues of fire, the gift of the Spirit, empowered the<|9.42|> <|9.42|> disciples to speak the languages of all who were gathered. God did not deliver a<|17.34|> <|17.34|> spirit that miraculously caused everyone to speak and understand the same language.<|26.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> God gifted a spirit that embraced difference and united people across cultures.<|7.36|> <|8.68|> God meets people where they are in ways they can understand.<|15.14|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> God's spirit of this dramatic and disruptive way is a reminder to us that difference was<|9.02|> <|9.02|> not the problem then and is not the problem now.<|14.36|> <|14.36|> The many ways we each show prejudice to difference in our hearts, in our words, and in our actions<|24.12|> <|24.12|> has always been the problem.<|26.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Peter then stands to address the question from the crowd: "What does this mean?"<|8.60|> <|8.60|> Many say that this is the first sermon in the church, Peter standing, citing Scripture<|16.04|> <|16.04|> to explain an act of God.<|19.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Not as charitably. Some also say this is clearly the first sermon because Peter's preaching about the event takes at least three times longer than the event.<|15.28|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This Peter, this recently Jesus-denying, impulsive Peter, stands before the people, empowered by the Holy Spirit, and delivers a prophetic sermon.<|16.94|> <|17.70|> Recalling Joel, he speaks of the gift of the Spirit to sons and daughters, men and women, and that everyone—<|28.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> everyone who calls on the name of the Lord in any language shall be saved.<|7.80|> <|8.80|> In receiving the Holy Spirit, if one can transcend differences,<|15.54|> <|16.28|> and it can make a prophetic preacher out of Peter, what can't it do?<|22.48|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This gift of the Holy Spirit, this day of Pentecost, is called by many the church's birthday.<|9.00|> <|9.00|> It is indeed the inauguration of the ability of Jesus' disciples to go out and preach and teach and heal and baptize.<|22.00|> <|22.00|> It is the day that everything about being a follower of Jesus changed.<|29.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.26|> Pentecost is about re-imagining<|5.30|> <|5.30|> what it is to be a follower of Jesus.<|8.88|> <|10.08|> We are living Pentecost, my friends.<|12.64|> <|14.52|> Jesus' disciples were no longer able<|17.66|> <|17.66|> to be with him physically.<|19.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.98|> Neither are we able to gather with each other physically.<|5.12|> <|6.50|> Jesus' disciples were scattered to create church in new ways.<|11.72|> <|13.30|> I'll tell you, seminary never prepared me to be a televangelist,<|17.38|> <|17.38|> and I'm guessing most of you never imagined sitting in your homes watching church on a Sunday either.<|26.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.06|> We are most certainly scattered.<|4.08|> <|5.12|> We find ourselves asking many of the same questions those early disciples asked themselves.<|13.62|> <|15.14|> So how are we, the church, in this new reality?<|20.74|> <|21.58|> What is our identity today?<|24.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> How do we go forward when everything seems to have changed?<|6.00|> <|7.68|> Just as this year's Lenten season called us to live Lent in ways we had never imagined,<|17.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Pentecost 2020 is forcing us to sit up, pay attention, and reimagine.<|9.30|> <|9.30|> Like a rush of violent wind accompanied by tongues as of fire which rested on each of the disciples,<|18.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Like the industrial fan my friend used to blast his congregation,<|5.84|> <|5.84|> how is the Holy Spirit blowing through our time and space?<|14.64|> <|14.64|> Pentecost is about reimagining church, a new way of being church.<|24.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> God was no longer embodied in the way the disciples knew.<|5.70|> <|6.80|> The body of Christ is no longer for a season gathered in a building.<|13.02|> <|14.24|> So empowered by the Holy Spirit, embracing difference and change, scattered, learning new ways—<|24.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> How are we being called to be the church?<|5.76|> <|7.52|> Be well, my friends.<|9.38|> <|10.20|> I miss you.<|11.14|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.00|><|endoftext|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This Peter, this recently Jesus-denying, impulsive Peter, stands before the people, empowered by the Holy Spirit, and delivers a prophetic sermon.<|16.94|>
<|17.70|> Recalling Joel, he speaks of the gift of the Spirit to sons and daughters, men and women, and that everyone—<|28.60|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> everyone who calls on the name of the Lord in any language shall be saved.<|7.80|>
<|8.80|> In receiving the Holy Spirit, if one can transcend differences,<|15.54|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This gift of the Holy Spirit, this day of Pentecost, is called by many the church's birthday.<|9.00|>
<|9.00|> It is indeed the inauguration of the ability of Jesus' disciples to go out and preach and teach and heal and baptize.<|22.00|>

<|7.68|> Just as this year's Lenten season called us to live Lent in ways we had never imagined,<|17.00|>
